nodes.body:
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Defer global AI quota until receipt validation passes**

Because `checkRateLimit` increments on every allowed check, this consumes the new global guest AI quota before the receipt is fetched or verified as a guest receipt. A single unauthenticated client can send requests for many random/nonexistent `receiptId`s and burn the shared `guest-process-global` budget without triggering any AI work, causing legitimate guest receipt processing to return 429 for the rest of the hour. Move the global quota check until after the receipt exists/is guest and the processing claim succeeds, or roll it back for non-processing failures.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Count only accepted uploads toward the global cap**

This increments the shared upload counter before the per-IP counter is checked, so once one IP has reached `GUEST_RATE_LIMIT`, its rejected upload attempts still continue consuming `GUEST_GLOBAL_LIMIT`. That lets a single client exhaust the global guest upload budget (even with invalid/no-file requests, since this runs before form validation) and block all other guests until the window resets; increment the global counter only after the request passes the per-IP and file validations, or undo it on rejection.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Derive login buckets only from trusted IP data**

When the app is exposed directly by the default Docker port mapping, or when a reverse proxy does not strip client-supplied forwarding headers, this accepts arbitrary `X-Forwarded-For` values from the login request. An attacker can rotate that header to get a fresh `login-ip:*` bucket on every request, so the new cross-account password-spraying limit is effectively bypassed; the existing guest-rate-limit code already treats forwarded-IP headers as spoofable in that deployment context. The login limiter needs to use a trusted source of the remote address or only honor these headers after an explicit trusted-proxy boundary.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

`parseInt(...)` here has no radix and no fallback when the env var is non-numeric. If `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_MAX` is accidentally set to a non-number, `parseInt` returns `NaN` and `checkRateLimit` effectively never blocks because comparisons against `NaN` are always false, silently disabling the limiter.

Same env parsing issue as the email limiter: without a radix + numeric fallback, a misconfigured `AUTH_IP_RATE_LIMIT_MAX` (e.g. empty or non-numeric) will become `NaN` and effectively disable the per-IP limiter.

`REGISTER_RATE_LIMIT_MAX` is parsed without a radix or fallback. If the env var is present but non-numeric, `parseInt` returns `NaN` and the registration rate limit will never trigger (because `entry.count >= NaN` is always false).

This comment is now inaccurate: `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_MAX` is used as a per-email limiter over 15 minutes (see `src/server/auth.ts`), not "per IP per hour". Leaving this as-is makes it easy to misconfigure rate limiting based on incorrect assumptions.

`getClientIp` trusts `x-forwarded-for`/`x-real-ip` directly. If ShareTab is deployed without a trusted reverse proxy that strips/sets these headers, an attacker can spoof them to bypass per-IP limits (undercutting the password-spraying protection this PR is adding). Consider either documenting/enforcing trusted-proxy behavior for these headers, or deriving IP from a more reliable header when available (e.g. `cf-connecting-ip`) and/or adding an additional global cap similar to the guest endpoints.

When the user has groups in different currencies, `dashboard.data.totalOwed/totalOwing` are still summed across groups (see `src/server/trpc/routers/balances.ts:164-189`), so displaying that aggregate number with a fallback currency of "USD" is misleading (it’s not converted). Consider suppressing the aggregate totals when `groupCurrencies.size > 1` (e.g. show "—" / "Mixed currencies") or changing the API to return aggregates per-currency.

This adds a new user-visible error toast string that bypasses next-intl, while the success toast uses `t("linkCopied")`. To keep the UI fully localized, consider adding a translation key (e.g. `split.claim.copyFailed`) and using `toast.error(t("copyFailed"))` (or similar) here.

This new error toast string is hard-coded (not localized). Since this page already uses next-intl (e.g. `tv(...)`) and other strings are translated, consider adding a translation key for clipboard failures and using that instead of an English literal.

The clipboard failure toast is currently hard-coded English. Since this dialog already uses next-intl (`t = useTranslations("groups")`), consider adding a translation key for copy failures and calling `toast.error(t("…"))` instead.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Filter saved equal-split users to current members**

When an equal-split expense still has shares for a real user who later left the group (the removal path preserves financial history), this seeds `selected` with that former member even though the checkbox list only renders current `members`. The user cannot unselect the hidden id, and `EqualSplit` emits it back in `shares`, so saving an otherwise unrelated edit fails server validation with “One or more share users are not members of this group.” Filter the saved ids against the current member ids before passing them into the split editor.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
